Treat Job Application as a Work Sample with Zinnov

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Pari Natarajan Pari Natarajan is an Influencer
1w
  • Report this post

Treat your job application as a work sample. With Zinnov's growth, the number of senior people reaching out to me for roles has increased. Unfortunately, several open with "I have 25/30 years of experience," then a paragraph about what they've done that could have gone to anyone. It's like listing a product's features and leaving the user to figure out how to use it. When we pitch consulting work, we don't lead with our CV. We study the company, form a view, and open with it. A job application is the same. If you can't show me how you think about my business before you join, why would I believe you'll think hard after? And with AI, there's no excuse. Fifteen minutes gets you a real read on a company. Skip it and it just says you're lazy and not curious. A simple structure to follow when you apply for a role: 1. Lead with one specific observation about the business. 2. Say what you'd do about it. 3. Then connect your experience to it. 4. Be direct about what you want. The job application is a work sample. Treat it that way. Zinnov

325 20 Comments
Like Comment
Achyut Menon "AK" 1w
  • Report this comment

Pari Natarajan, this took me back to a conversation I had years ago with a hiring manager who said: "Achyut, I don't want to know what he has done. I want to know how he thinks." I have seen this pattern play out thousands of times in search. The candidate who opens with "28 years, FMCG, P&L of ₹800 crores" - generic. Could be anyone. The one who opens with "I noticed your APAC revenue hasn't moved in three years despite the category growing- here's what I think is happening and what I'd do in the first 90 days" - that person gets the meeting. Every time. What you've described, Pari, is actually the oldest sales truth in the book. Nobody buys features. They buy the feeling that you understand their problem better than they do. A job application is a pitch deck. The hiring manager is the investor. And nobody funds a pitch that starts with the founder's bio. With AI now giving everyone a 15-minute deep-dive into any company, there's no excuse to walk in cold. If you still do it - that's not a skill gap. That's a curiosity gap. Your four steps are so impactful. Simple. Rarely done. Always noticed

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Phani Pattamatta 1w
  • Report this comment

Totally agree Pari. 😊 The two most important hiring documents, a résumé and a job description, are both rooted in the past, yet they’re used to predict success in a future role. Having spent two decades in the talent industry helping clients solve hiring challenges, I often wonder why we continue to rely so heavily on backward-looking indicators to make forward-looking decisions. 😅 Even more so today, when predicting how work itself will evolve is becoming nearly impossible - even for the most informed and intelligent among us. 😂

Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Kaustubh Patekar 1w
  • Report this comment

Interesting take. Tailoring an application is a fair ask. The flip side of it is that hiring teams also ask for a lot of strategy work upfront and often ignore the candidate after that. That needs to be avoided as well.

Nishant Kashyap 1w
  • Report this comment

Before submitting a consulting proposal one does extensive discussions and interviews with the client to tailor a solution. Similarly, companies and hiring managers abroad are very open to informational interviews by candidates. Wonder if you have that culture and time?

3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Vishal Patel 3d
  • Report this comment

Pari Natarajan the write looks impressive but is impractical, if I may be direct. All accepts applications over platforms like LinkedIn and go through ATS. These platforms ask for what candidate has done and that what you as recruiter get. So suggesting to mention what candidate has observed in the business and how can he help is remote. This can work only with cold emails to recruiters like you. And be honest, how many emails you even read or say can read. So all these seem very good on a LinkedIn post but the reality is different. Do you think your recruitment team is trained to first capture such info from candidates? Because candidates don't have a way to provide such info today. Show me a single one. Even if there is, recruiters are ATS slaves. They don't read, ATS does the filtering. So on what basis, you are writing such post, is a big question. 😀

Reply
1 Reaction
Libin Mathew 1w
  • Report this comment

Pari Natarajan If that would be the case, people who apply 1000s of application daily, need to spend extra hours to study each company and application. And does it really matter? No, Each engineer is going to build product irrespective of which domain it is for. Just like for doctor, they should know how to treat patient, and rest info about hospital is just add-on. Either each company should get rid of basic questionaire on job application request from LinkedIn or other forum, instead ask relevant aspect they looking for? And make candidate invest time effort in preparing the answer. Which definitely will limit the infinte Easy Apply Click counts. And only most suitable candidate will apply. Pari Natarajan if company pay me well, I will show them additional interest and all the missing aspects of their business too.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions
Steve Dantas 1w
  • Report this comment

Best advice ever, Pari. In this hyper-competitive, apocalyptic AI future, the ones that go above and beyond will succeed. Past successes are in the past. How can you help win business? How can you help bring in customers? What significant insight does your experience add to the current business problem? Can you roll up your sleeves and dive right in, instead of simply pontificating? Do you know any other top performers you can bring along? These and many other such questions require answers that companies are looking for. A resume is just one artefact today.

Nice thought process that adds value to both sir.. applicants should be thinking in these lines 👏👍

Deepak Vijaykumar 1w

The disconnect between experience and relevance can be staggering. reminding candidates that their application isn't just a formality but a demonstration of problem-solving can shift the narrative. how do you see this evolving with younger generations entering the workforce?

More Relevant Posts

  • Talent Pixel

    5 followers

    2w Edited
    • Report this post

    7.4 seconds. That's how long your résumé gets before a yes or no. A page can't capture how you think — but your "signal" can. Signal is the evidence of how you work that lives outside the résumé. Here's what to send, and how to do it well: 1. Show the thinking, not just the result.  Don't say "grew revenue 30%." Show the decision: what you saw, what you bet on, what you'd do differently. People hire judgment, not outcomes. 2. Build something small and public.  A teardown, a how-I-did-it post, a mini case study, a repo, a Loom. One concrete artifact beats ten bullet points. 3. Leave a trail.  Comment with substance on your field. Answer the questions others skip. Over months, that trail becomes proof you actually know your space. 4. Make your work easy to verify.  Link it. A portfolio, a doc, a demo. "Trust me" is weak signal; "here, look" is strong. 5. Be specific about the room you want to be in. Vague gets ignored. Name the problem you want to solve and who you want to solve it for. The résumé gets you 7 seconds. Signal gets you remembered. 👉 What's the best signal you've ever sent — or received? Follow Talent Pixel for weekly hiring insights & views. #JobSearch #CareerGrowth #PersonalBranding #FutureOfWork #Hiring #TalentPixel

    Every proposal won’t be accepted. Every application won’t get a response. Every idea won’t gain immediate support. But it only takes one opportunity, one client, one partnership, or one decision-maker to change the trajectory of your career or business. Stay persistent. Stay prepared. Keep knocking. The right YES is often worth every NO that came before it. #HopeThisHelpsSomeoneToday #Solutionsbytravistech

  • Valentin Zlydnev
    6d
    • Report this post

    𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝗜 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗱 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗼𝗿 "𝗻𝗮𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁𝘀" 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗜 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺𝘀. I spent years interviewing hundreds of tech professionals. While most managers focused purely on a candidate's specific tech stack, I was hunting for something else: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝘆𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗵. I prioritized specialists with a broad horizon because of a simple Contrast:  • 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗮𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄 𝗦𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁: Knew their database inside and out, but became a bottleneck the moment a problem required understanding business logic or user psychology  • 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝘆𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗵: Understood the code, but also had an "excited curiosity" for things like design, marketing, or history I looked for the developer who understood typography or was a fan of some movie franchise. These candidates didn't just solve tickets; they solved problems. Just as Steve Jobs used a random calligraphy class to revolutionize computer typography, my best hires used their "broad interests" to find edges that others missed. Even though I am not currently hiring, this lesson stayed with me: 𝗕𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗱 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄𝗹𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗶𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗮 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻—𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺-𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴. 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗼𝗻𝗲 "𝘂𝗻𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱" 𝗵𝗼𝗯𝗯𝘆 𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘂𝗻𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗲𝗱𝗹𝘆 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗷𝗼𝗯? I’d love to hear your stories in the comments—let’s see how many "hidden polymaths" are in my network!

  • Alex A
    3w
    • Report this post

    Over the past few months, we've had several new conversations that started with a referral from an existing client. Like most companies, we invest in marketing. We work on our website, create content, attend industry events, and look for ways to connect with the right people. But when someone recommends an engineering partner, they're usually not thinking about any of that. They're thinking about their experience. Did the team understand the problem? Was communication clear? Did they provide practical solutions? Could they adapt when requirements changed or unexpected challenges came up? In manufacturing and industrial environments, projects rarely follow a straight line. Priorities shift, constraints emerge, and decisions often affect multiple teams and disciplines. That's why many of our conversations start with understanding the bigger picture before discussing deliverables. What are you trying to achieve? What's creating the bottleneck? What constraints are you working within? The referrals we've received recently have been a good reminder of something that's easy to overlook. For all the conversations around marketing, branding, and business development, many opportunities still begin with one person sharing a positive experience with another. That's what makes referrals meaningful. We're grateful to every client who has recommended us. We appreciate the confidence that comes with it. #Engineering #Manufacturing #ClientRelationships #BusinessDevelopment #EngineeringDesign

  • Kayla Griffith
    1w
    • Report this post

    Job searching in today’s market isn’t easy, especially early in your career. AI tools can help take some of the pressure off when used thoughtfully, as long as they support your work rather than replace it. Here are a few ways AI can help you in your job search and where it’s important to draw the line. ✅ AI search tools can be great for finding organizations with openings that are of interest and relevant to you, and they can also help you identify jobs and careers that match your skillset outside of what you're familiar with... 🛑 but do NOT use them to mass apply to a high volume of openings without your review and input. ✅ Leveraging AI writing tools to help polish your drafts and tailor your application can help you refresh your materials or adapt your resume for Applicant Tracking Systems... 🛑 but don't use generative AI to fully write your resume or cover letters, and never submit unedited, unreviewed content. ✅ An AI tool can be useful when preparing for interviews by doing basic research on the organization and suggesting interview questions based on the company website and job description... 🛑 but you should never use AI tools during an interview or skills assessment without permissions from the interviewer or recruiter. As AI becomes a greater presence in our day-to-day lives, it can become easy to rely on it more and more. But at the end of the day, it's you, not the LLM, who is getting hired for the role. So, if you choose to use these tools, it's essential you do so thoughtfully and ensure you're representing yourself and your skills authentically. Sign up for our Talent Community Newsletter for more insights like this plus new opportunities and what it’s really like to work at the Allen Institute! https://lnkd.in/gfEgk7uz

  • Sumaiya Shaikh
    4w
    • Report this post

    I’ve been thinking about what really connects people and technology… I didn’t start my career in tech. I started in conversations — real ones. The kind where someone comes to you with a question, a worry, or a moment of uncertainty… and you realise your job isn’t just to answer. It’s to listen. Working with people taught me something I’ve carried into every role since: most problems aren’t technical — they’re human. They need patience, clarity, and someone who genuinely cares. Then I discovered code. A different language, but the same purpose: solve problems, make things easier, bring clarity where there’s confusion. And suddenly my world made sense. I didn’t have to choose between people and technology — I could bridge them. Today, whether I’m handling an enquiry, supporting a client, or building a solution, my approach is the same: Listen first. Understand deeply. Solve with empathy. Communicate with clarity. Because behind every ticket, every case, every “quick question”… there’s a person who just wants to feel heard. If there’s one thing I’ve learned, it’s this: "The most meaningful work isn’t about systems or tools — it’s about the humans who rely on them." And that’s the space I want to keep showing up in. If your work is about bridging people and systems too, I’d love to connect. #CareerJourney #EmpathyInTech #PublicService #Communication #ProblemSolving #HumanCenteredDesign #EnquiriesOfficer #TechForGood

  • Brandon Henderson
    1w
    • Report this post

    I want to ask you something real. If your company called you into a meeting tomorrow and told you it was your last day - what would you do? Most people I talk to in tech have never seriously answered that question when their job felt secure. But the people who have thought about it - really thought about it - are the ones who start seeing their career differently. - less like a job and more like something they actually own. That is the conversation happening inside The Quiet Pivot right now. If that question made you uncomfortable even for a second - you might want to come check it out. It is free. It is real people. And nobody is selling shortcuts. Link in the comments.

  • Dale Cooper
    2d
    • Report this post

    I've stopped starting with "How?" Now I start with "𝗪𝗵𝘆?" Purpose is the "𝘄𝗵𝘆." 𝗪𝗵𝘆 does this opportunity matter? Does it move the band closer to what we're trying to build? Does it put us in front of the right audience? Does it create opportunities beyond today? When the why is clear enough, the answers and the resources begin to appear. Only then does it make sense to ask: Should we do it? And if the answer is yes... How do we make it happen? Too often we get caught up solving the "how" before we've defined the purpose. I've found the strongest decisions begin with "𝘄𝗵𝘆."

    Like Comment

  • MobiusEngine

    95,374 followers

    2w
    • Report this post

    Sarah, a senior product manager, was on her fourth take-home assignment in two months. The latest ask was a full 90-day go-to-market strategy for a new product, complete with financial projections. She spent 20 hours over a weekend perfecting the deck, convinced this was the only way to stand out in the hyper-competitive 2025 job market. After submitting, she received a two-sentence rejection email saying they went with an internal candidate. Your willingness to question an unreasonable assignment is the REAL interview. This is the problem MobiusEngine.ai was built for.

    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Ash Wilkin
    1mo
    • Report this post

    This morning I was working through applications for a Head of Product role I'm hiring for at Solvace. Alongside the applications were the follow-up emails, LinkedIn messages and connection requests that have become part of the process. At the moment, I'm probably receiving close to 100 direct messages a day from candidates who have applied. That's not a complaint - people are investing time in opportunities they're excited about, and I genuinely respect that. What I found myself thinking about, though, was how difficult it's becoming to distinguish genuine interest from well-written interest. A lot of the messages say the right things. Candidates tell me they connect with the mission, that they're excited by the opportunity, that the company's values resonate with them, and that they'd love to be part of the journey. The challenge is that when you read dozens of these messages back-to-back, they begin to sound remarkably similar. That's not because the people are similar. It's because we've all become very good at producing the language of enthusiasm. The messages that stay with me are rarely the most polished ones. They're usually from people who have taken the time to share an observation about the product, challenge an assumption, reference something specific they've seen, or tell a story from their own experience that relates to the role. They don't spend much time telling me they're interested. They show me why they became interested in the first place. It feels like we're entering a world where authenticity isn't about avoiding AI or writing the perfect message. It's about contributing something that could only have come from you. A perspective. An experience. An insight. A question. The irony is that as it becomes easier than ever to generate impressive-sounding communication, genuinely human communication becomes far more memorable. For anyone applying for roles right now, I'd be interested to know your thoughts. Has the application process changed the way you approach outreach? And for recruiters and hiring managers, what actually catches your attention these days?

    27 8 Comments
    Like Comment
